summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the correct order of the four phases of mitosis?

The mnemonic PMAT helps remember the order: Prophase (chromosomes condense, spindle forms), Metaphase (chromosomes align at the equator), Anaphase (sister chromatids separate), Telophase (nuclear envelopes reform). Cytokinesis (cytoplasm division) typically overlaps with telophase.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: Which definition BEST describes the 'cleavage furrow'?

The cleavage furrow is the mechanism of cytokinesis in animal cells. A contractile ring assembles just beneath the plasma membrane at the cell equator (positioned by signals from the mitotic spindle midzone). The ring is composed of actin filaments and myosin II motor proteins that contract, pulling the membrane inward like a drawstring purse until the cell pinches into two daughter cells.

Example: In a biology lab, students examine an onion root tip under a microscope and observe cells in different stages. They count 20 cells in interphase, 10 in prophase, 6 in metaphase, 3 in anaphase, and 5 in telophase. What can they conclude about the relative duration of each phase?

This is a classic mitotic index experiment. In a rapidly dividing tissue like the onion root tip meristem, the proportion of cells observed in each phase reflects the duration of that phase (assuming cells are asynchronous). More cells in interphase means it takes the longest. Among mitotic phases, prophase has the most cells (longest mitotic phase), and anaphase has the fewest (shortest phase, ~3–5 minutes).

Example: In a biology lab, students prepare slides from an onion root tip and count cells in each phase of mitosis. Out of 200 cells observed, 120 are in interphase, 40 in prophase, 20 in metaphase, 10 in anaphase, and 10 in telophase. Students then examine a slide from a fast-growing tumor and find that 100 of 200 cells are actively in mitotic phases. What does the comparison of mitotic index (percentage of cells in mitosis) tell us?

The mitotic index is calculated as (cells in mitosis / total cells counted) × 100%. For the onion root tip: 80 mitotic cells / 200 = 40%. For the tumor: 100 / 200 = 50%. A higher mitotic index indicates a greater proportion of cells are actively dividing. Pathologists use the mitotic index as a diagnostic tool — a high mitotic index in a tissue biopsy can indicate cancer or other conditions with abnormal cell proliferation. Normal tissues like root tips have high mitotic indices because they are regions of active growth (meristems).

Example: Frog embryos undergo extremely rapid cleavage divisions after fertilization, with the first 12 divisions occurring in about 8 hours without any cell growth between divisions. Each successive cell (blastomere) gets smaller. Why can these early embryonic cells skip the G₁ and G₂ growth phases that most somatic cells require?

Early embryonic cleavage divisions in frogs (Xenopus) are among the fastest cell cycles known (~30 minutes each). The enormous egg (~1.2 mm diameter) is pre-loaded with vast stores of maternal mRNAs, proteins, cyclins, histones, ribosomes, and yolk nutrients during oogenesis. These stockpiled resources allow the embryo to simply alternate between S phase (DNA replication) and M phase (mitosis) without G₁ or G₂ gaps. The result is rapid subdivision of the large egg into thousands of progressively smaller blastomeres, forming a hollow blastula. After about 12 divisions (~4,096 cells), the mid-blastula transition (MBT) activates the embryo's own genome, and normal G₁/G₂ phases resume.
